data.title: Cerebras backs away from IPO after $1.1B fundraising round
data.description:
Cerebras Systems (CRBS) has withdrawn plans for an initial public offering, more than a year after first signaling its intention to go public. The company disclosed the decision in a filing late Friday, saying it would not pursue the IPO outlined in earlier documents, which had never taken effect.

The move comes just days after the AI chipmaker and data center operator secured $1.1 billion in fresh capital, giving it a valuation of $8.1 billion. Chief Executive Andrew Feldman said at the time that the new funding did not change Cerebras’ (CRBS) long-term ambitions for a listing.

The fundraising adds to a surge of investment into artificial intelligence infrastructure, with investors betting the technology will reshape global industries. It also follows a stretch of renewed IPO activity in the U.S., where firms such as Netskope (NTSK) and Figure Technology Solutions (FIGR) have rallied more than 20% since their market debuts.

Cerebras (CRBS) originally filed for a public offering in September 2024, but the effort stalled amid a federal review of its ties with Abu Dhabi AI company G42. The Committee on Foreign Investment in the U.S. concluded its review earlier this year, with Cerebras (CRBS) announcing in March that outstanding issues had been resolved.

The latest round was led by Fidelity Management & Research and Atreides Management, with participation from Tiger Global, Valor Equity Partners and 1789 Capital, the venture firm linked to Donald Trump Jr. Existing backers Altimeter Capital and Benchmark also joined the raise.

data.title: Federal job cuts add strain to weakening labor market in U.S.
data.description:
Roughly 100,000 federal employees officially left the government payroll this week, deepening concerns about the softening U.S. job market, The Wall Street Journal reported Saturday. The departures coincide with an ongoing government shutdown that could bring additional losses.

The cuts stem from the administration’s deferred-resignation program, which allowed employees to leave but continue receiving pay and benefits for months. About 154,000 took the deal, according to the Office of Personnel Management, with most staying on the books through Sept. 30. Combined with a hiring freeze, voluntary exits and layoffs, the federal workforce is expected to shrink by hundreds of thousands this year.

President Trump has portrayed the reductions as part of a broader effort to streamline what he calls an “unaccountable” bureaucracy. Analysts, however, warn the downsizing adds another drag on a labor market already showing weakness across industries from finance to manufacturing. The federal job cuts are one of the reasons why the labor market has weakened in recent months, Ryan Sweet, chief U.S. economist at Oxford Economics, told the Journal.

For many employees, the resignation program felt less like a choice than a necessity. Former USDA official Cynthia Iglesias Guven said office tensions grew after many of her projects were scrapped, leading her to accept the April buyout. In Huntsville, Ala., Army aviation staffer Rick Beevers worried his probationary status would make him vulnerable to layoffs and opted for the deal.

The job search hasn’t been easy for all. Applications from former federal workers surged more than 40% this year on Indeed, according to the platform, while some career coaches say many buyout recipients have yet to fully reenter the job hunt.

The shutdown could worsen the picture. Furloughed staff may be counted as unemployed in official data, while research from several universities suggests federal employees are significantly more likely to quit voluntarily within a year after a shutdown.

data.title: Renault reportedly weighing up to 3,000 job cuts
data.description:
Renault SA (OTCPK:RNSDF) (OTCPK:RNLSY) is planning to slash up to 3,000 positions globally as part of a major cost-cutting initiative, multiple news outlets reported Saturday, citing the French news site l’Informe.

Citing people familiar with the matter, l’Informe said that the French automaker has yet to formally authorize the plans, and a final decision on the proposed job cuts is expected by the end of this year. The job losses will be widespread, with the company’s global sites, including its headquarters near Paris, set to take a hit.

The layoffs, being considered under a restructuring program called “Arrow,” are expected to affect the company's support functions, including HR, finance, and marketing, impacting roughly 15% of employees serving those departments.

In a statement, Renault SA (OTCPK:RNSDF) confirmed it is reviewing potential changes at the company, including ways to simplify and speed up operations due to intense rivalry and uncertainties in the auto market.

“Given the uncertainties in the automotive market and the extremely competitive environment, we confirm that we are considering ways to simplify, speed up execution, and optimize our fixed costs,” Renault (OTCPK:RNLSY) noted.

The proposed layoffs follow the company’s recent full-year guidance cut, which sent its Paris-listed shares ~18% lower in July, marking its biggest intraday decline in more than five years.

data.title: S&P 500 earnings growth to slow in Q3, but likely to beat expectations: Goldman Sachs
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) enters third-quarter earnings season next week, with results expected from nearly three-quarters of the index’s market capitalization by the end of October, according to Goldman Sachs strategist David Kostin. Nvidia (NVDA), the benchmark’s largest stock, is set to report on November 19.

Consensus forecasts call for S&P 500 earnings growth to slow to 6% year-over-year, down from 11% in the second quarter. In an Oct. 4 report to clients, Kostin said he expects results will come in stronger, noting that “earnings growth will surpass this forecast, as usual, due to stronger sales growth than consensus expects and positive surprises from the Magnificent 7.”

Still, he acknowledged that EPS growth will decelerate modestly compared with last quarter given a smaller currency tailwind, higher tariff payments and the absence of a one-time gain that boosted prior results.

Sales and tariff pressures

Analysts anticipate S&P 500 sales growth will fall to 4% in the third quarter from 6% in the second. Kostin argued that view “appears too conservative” given economic data, with Goldman economists estimating nominal GDP rose 4.7% year-over-year, essentially unchanged from the prior quarter’s 4.6%.

Tariffs, however, emerged as a larger drag on profits. Customs duties reached $93 billion in the third quarter, a 33% jump from the second. While companies likely held margins steady, Goldman does not expect significant expansion.

Magnificent 7 and AI capex

Expectations for the Magnificent 7 remain subdued. Analysts project EPS growth of 14% in the third quarter, roughly half the pace achieved in the first half of 2025. Kostin noted that estimates have repeatedly underestimated mega-cap results, setting a “low bar” again this quarter.

AI-related capital expenditures are also under scrutiny. Consensus implies hyperscaler capex will grow 75% year-over-year in the third quarter, before slowing sharply to 42% in the fourth and to about 20% in 2026. However, Kostin highlighted that “AI capex spending has consistently exceeded bottom-up estimates during the last several quarters,” making hyperscaler commentary on AI demand and investment a focal point for investors this season.

data.title: Elon Musk says Tesla FSD 14 launch postponed to Monday
data.description:
As Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) is set to roll out its Full Self Driving model v14, Elon Musk, CEO of the EV maker, says that a technical glitch surfaced at the last moment, forcing his company to delay the launch until Monday.

“Last minute bug cropped up with V14. Released is pushed to Monday but that gives us time to add a few more features,” the billionaire investor wrote on X, replying to another X user who questioned about the launch timeline.

Last week, Musk announced that the latest iterations of Tesla’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) subscription-based FSD software will be launched in stages from this week. “Version 14.0 goes into early wide release next week, then 14.1 about 2 weeks later, and finally 14.2,” he said, noting that “the car will feel almost like it is sentient being by 14.2.”

FSD hasn’t undergone any significant upgrades since its v13 launch late last year. In August, Musk claimed that the v14 launch would mark the second biggest update to FSD after v12.

data.title: Is Apple leaving money on the table? Needham thinks so
data.description:
If you ask most people about the cost of the iPhone, they're likely to say they're expensive, but provide good value for the money. Some might even say Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) has enriched their lives with its iconic device.

However, investment firm Needham believes that Apple is no longer the innovative company it once was and that simply “good enough” is hurting it and its shareholders. 

The price of the iPhone has not kept up with inflation, Needham analyst Laura Martin said. As such, shareholders have had to eat an opportunity cost of 13% based on her perception of a lack of innovation. 

“Every year, AAPL spends many hours telling analysts, investors and consumers about new features, tools, capabilities, cameras, and colors,” Martin wrote in a note to clients. “However, Wall Street bases valuation on revenue growth and [free cash flow]. Since 2019, same-store iPhone prices have not kept up with inflation. We assume that AAPL would raise prices if consumers would tolerate it. We link AAPL's inability to increase prices to its inability to introduce new killer apps and differentiated features that would give it pricing power above inflation.”

Martin has a Hold rating on Apple shares and no price target.

Apple unveiled the latest iPhone lineup last month, including the three core models — the iPhone 17, iPhone 17 Pro and iPhone 17 Pro Max. It also announced the iPhone Air, a slimmed-down smartphone that many have praised as an example of Apple's ability to keep innovating.

However, that may not be enough for the investment community, given that Martin believes Apple has lost pricing power (the iPhone Air starts at $999), which she believes many on Wall Street defined as “the ability to increase prices faster than inflation.”

Even though the iPhone Air received plenty of praise from the media, reviewers and industry analysts, consumers may not be responding in the same way. Early sales data suggests that the iPhone 17 and iPhone 17 Pro are outselling the iPhone Air, according to Bank of America. 

“Our investment conclusion is that AAPL's lack of innovation has lowered public shareholder value by 13% over the past 6 years (2019-2025), through its inability to raise prices on its 3 core same-store iPhone models by enough to keep pace with inflation,” Martin added.

data.title: Critical Metals soars as Trump administration weighs taking stake - Reuters
data.description:
Critical Metals (NASDAQ:CRML) +75.2% post-market Friday following a Reuters report that Trump administration officials have discussed taking a stake in the company whose flagship project, the Tanbreez mine in Greenland, is one of the world's largest rare earth deposits.

Critical Metals (NASDAQ:CRML), which agreed to buy the Tanbreez deposit last year for $5 million in cash and $211 million in stock, applied in June for a $50 million grant through the Defense Production Act, but the Trump administration has begun discussions with the company about converting the grant into an equity stake, according to the report.

If the deal goes through, a $50 million conversion would mean a ~8% stake in the company, although negotiations are not final and the deal could collapse.

The equity stake reportedly would be separate from a $120 million loan under consideration by the U.S. Export-Import Bank to help the company develop Tanbreez, which is expected to cost $290 million to bring into commercial production.

Critical Metals (CRML) said earlier this week it amended its acquisition agreement with Rimbal Pty Ltd. allowing it to increase its ownership stake in Tanbreez from 42% to 92.5% in exchange for the issuance of ~14.5 million common shares of the company.

The administration recently has taken equity stakes in Lithium Americas and MP Materials, highlighting Trump's desire for the U.S. to benefit from growing production of rare earth minerals.

data.title: Chevron oil refinery fire could raise California pump prices by a dollar, analyst says
data.description:
California motorists could pay nearly a dollar more per gallon for gasoline after a fire erupted last night at Chevron's (NYSE:CVX) El Segundo refinery near Los Angeles, GasBuddy energy analyst Patrick De Haan said Friday.

"This is likely to be a significant problem and potentially for months or more," De Haan wrote. "This refinery can handle nearly 300k bpd of crude oil and is the largest refinery on the west coast. Preliminary *guess* is #gasprices could rise 35-95c/gal on the West coast."

The refinery supplies jet fuel for the Los Angeles International Airport, and supplies 20% of all motor vehicle fuels and 40% of the jet fuel consumed in southern California.

The fire comes as California grapples with rapidly declining oil production, which has pushed Gov. Newsom to sign into law a legislative package that included a measure that would approve thousands of new oil drilling permits in Kern County.

Crude oil futures snapped a four-session losing streak Friday, but it was not enough to avoid a sharp weekly loss ahead of an expected OPEC+ production increase when the group meets this weekend, and Iraq has resumed oil exports from Kurdistan via Turkey after being shut in the past two years.

"Add in the bearish EIA storage data from earlier this week and it's hard to be bullish crude in the near term," BOK Financial's Dennis Kissler said in a note.

J.P. Morgan analysts said they believed September marked a turning point, with the oil market heading towards a sizeable surplus in Q4 and into next year.

It is not clear how oil markets will respond to breaking news from the Middle East, where Hamas said it would agree to some aspects of President Trump's plan to end the Gaza war, including releasing all Israeli hostages, but that it would seek negotiations over many of its other terms.

Both major crude oil benchmarks rose 0.6% on Friday, but for the week, front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for November delivery finished -7.3% to $60.88/bbl and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for December delivery closed -6.8% to $64.53/bbl.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) extended the previous session's loss but still logged a second straight weekly gain, with the front-month Nymex November contract down 3.4% on Friday to end the week +3.7% at $3.324/MMBtu.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (NYSEARCA:UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (NYSEARCA:XLE)

Energy stocks, as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLE), ended the week -3.3%.

Top 20 gainers in energy and natural resources in the past 5 days: Plug Power (PLUG) +60.7%, USA Rare Earth (UAR) +49.8%, Lithium Americas (LAC) +42.8%, Spruce Power (SPRU) +38.4%, American Resources (AREC) +33.1%, Peabody Energy (BTU) +30.4%, FuelCell Energy (FCEL) +29.2%, Bloom Energy (BE) +28.4%, Ballard Power (BLDP) +28.3%, Tamboran Resources (TBN) +28.2%, Eos Energy Enterprises (EOSE) +24.5%, U.S. Antimony (AUMY) +24%, Critical Metals (CRML) +22.9%, Montauk Renewables (MNTK) +22.7%, Ramaco Resources (METC) +20.1%.

Top 5 decliners in energy and natural resources in the past 5 days: OMS Energy Technologies (OMSE) -18.2%, Amplify Energy (AMPY) -17.3%, Kinetik Holdings (KNTK) -15.4%, ASP Isotopes (ASPI) -11.5%, Marine Petroleum (MARPS) -11.2%.

Source: Barchart.com

data.title: Braskem taps entire $1B credit line amid debt review - Bloomberg
data.description:
Braskem (NYSE:BAK) has tapped its entire $1 billion credit facility amid a downturn in Brazil's chemical industry and mounting investor concerns over the troubled company, Bloomberg reported Friday.

The unsecured facility with a syndicate of banks is due to expire late next year, and the company has been in talks to renew the line, trying to avoid having to include collateral, according to the report.

Braskem (NYSE:BAK) took investors by surprise last week when it disclosed that it hired advisors to help prepare a "diagnosis of economic and financial alternatives to optimize its capital structure," which was interpreted as a signal that a debt restructuring was on the way.

Petrobras' (PBR) board has begun strategizing how to position itself if Braskem (BAK) is restructured, including potentially injecting capital into the company, Bloomberg reported earlier this week; Braskem is controlled by Petrobras and Novono.
